summary: Julia Evans describes how anonymous talk review worked surprisingly well for !!Con, a conference she helped organize. Despite initial fears about not knowing speakers' identities, the process produced a diverse range of high-quality talks from both experienced and new speakers. The anonymization required significant effort from a dedicated team member who personally scrubbed all proposals. The result was a process where speakers felt confident they were judged on merit, and organizers couldn't give preferential treatment to famous speakers or friends.
key_points: Anonymous review required significant manual effort — one person anonymized all proposals and then recused himself from the review process entirely
The letter grade system (A-D) with advocate requirements created a structured way to winnow 168 proposals down to 24 accepted talks
Anonymous review prevented both positive bias (accepting talks from famous people or friends) and negative bias (judging on demographics rather than proposal quality)
Speakers specifically expressed relief at knowing they were selected on merit rather than identity characteristics
The organizers sent personalized rejection emails highlighting what they liked about each rejected proposal
money_quotes: I was honestly a bit scared by this – how were we going to make sure people were good at public speaking if we didn't even know who they were?! But it turned out my fears were totally unfounded.
It's a relief to know that I wasn't picked for gender, race, age, or anything like that.
It was impossible to accept less-interesting-sounding talks just because they were 'by someone famous'.
It was impossible to give preferential treatment to our friends! This was a bit sad, but it made the conference better.
Each of us assigned a proposal a letter grade (A-D), from 'I will fight for this talk', to 'I will fight for this talk to be rejected'.
None of this is to say that anonymous review is the best way to do talk review. But it worked very well for us.
key_insight: Anonymous talk review, while labor-intensive, produces fairer outcomes by forcing organizers to judge proposals purely on merit and giving speakers confidence that their selection was earned.
